payload.answer: Store durable memory for the table as schema/metadata (columns id/name/value), plus aggregates or a small sample; keep the full 500-row dataset out of durable memory unless the user requires long-term row-level retrieval, in which case reference an external attachment/cache by ID and selectively promote needed rows later.
payload.decision: Do not store the full repeated 500-row CSV verbatim as durable memory. Store durable metadata (schema/column meanings), a small representative sample or aggregated statistics, and keep the full dataset as ephemeral/cache or external attachment referenced by an ID, unless the user explicitly needs row-level retrieval long-term.

entity_resolution.reason: No pre-existing entities were referenced; only generic policy and storage guidance is being captured.

payload.intent: Decide how a large CSV table (500 rows) should be stored in Brain’s personal memory system.

memory_cards.statement: For large tabular inputs with many repeated rows, store durable memory as schema/metadata plus aggregates or a small representative sample, not the entire dataset verbatim.

When exact row-level values may be needed later, store the full dataset outside durable memory (ephemeral/cache or external attachment) and reference it by an ID; then optionally materialize only the specific rows later as separate durable memory items.

Use normalized entity/value memory: persist column meanings (e.g., id, name, value) and treat each distinct (name, value) pattern or (id → name/value) mapping as separate entities only if it supports future queries.

details: Classified as large repeated tabular input where verbatim durable storage is unnecessary.
Provided a storage strategy balancing durability with query needs.

repair_options: If the user later confirms row-level lookup is needed, promote only the relevant subset of rows (or distinct key mappings) into durable memory.
If repeated patterns are meaningful, store aggregated counts by (name,value) or by other stable keys instead of storing all rows.
If provenance matters, attach the original CSV as an external artifact and store only a reference plus derived metadata.
